intro: You are the student council president for a week. Real problems arrive daily.
objective: Balance student needs, teacher requests, budget limits, and fairness.

nodes.text: Day 1: Students want longer break. Teachers want shorter. What do you decide?

choices.title: Survey both sides and propose a compromise
Side with teachers for discipline
Side with students for happiness
choices.description: Comfort first, but lose teacher respect.

nodes.text: Day 2: A teacher reports broken lab equipment. Replacement costs 2000. Budget is tight.

choices.title: Repair what is possible, replace critical only
Buy new equipment immediately
Delay and use theory classes instead

nodes.text: Day 3: A student is caught bullying. Parents demand no punishment. Victim is scared.

choices.title: Counseling for both, clear school policy
Suspend the bully immediately
Ignore and let them sort it out
choices.description: Avoid conflict, but enable future harm.

nodes.text: Day 4: A water pipe bursts. School may close tomorrow.
Day 4 continued: How do you handle the crisis?

choices.title: Coordinate staff, shift classes, arrange water
Declare holiday and fix properly
Ask students to bring water bottles

nodes.text: Day 5: Final review. A parent offers a donation for preferential treatment of their child.

choices.title: Decline politely, explain fairness
Accept but treat everyone equally
choices.description: Take money, hope no one notices.

choices.title: Accept and give their child privileges

nodes.text: Your week ends. The school still stands. Some decisions echo longer than others.
reflection: Which decision was hardest because it had no perfect answer?
What is the relationship between fairness and leadership?

intro: You are the youth coordinator for a small village. Water, crops, school, and health all need attention.
objective: Improve the village over a full season without breaking any single system.

nodes.text: Season 1: Water is scarce. How do you manage it?

choices.title: Build a rainwater harvesting system
Dig deeper wells immediately
Ration water strictly by household

nodes.text: Season 2: Crops are failing. Pests are spreading. What is your priority?

choices.title: Introduce mixed cropping and organic pest control
Buy chemical pesticides and fertilizers
Focus on one crop and maximize it
choices.description: Specialization, but risk if it fails.

nodes.text: Season 3: The school needs a teacher. The clinic needs a nurse. You have funds for one.

choices.title: Fund the teacher, train a local health volunteer
Fund the nurse, use older students to teach younger
Split the money and hire both part-time
choices.description: Both get attention, but neither is full.

nodes.text: Unexpected drought: The harvest is 40% below expectation.
Drought impact: Food shortage is real. What do you do?

choices.title: Request government aid and organize community kitchens
Buy food from neighboring village
Let families handle their own shortage

nodes.text: Season 4: You have one remaining project. What do you invest in for the future?

choices.title: Solar-powered irrigation
Village library and internet
Save the money for emergencies
choices.description: Safety buffer, but no new growth.

nodes.text: One year passes. The village is different. Some systems are stronger. Some are still fragile. That is management.
reflection: Which system was most connected to all others?
Why is saving sometimes more strategic than spending?

intro: You and a small team are stranded on an island. Resources are limited. Weather is unpredictable.
objective: Survive, signal for rescue, and maintain team health and morale.

nodes.text: Hour 1: You find a small amount of fresh water, some food, and basic tools. What do you prioritize?

choices.title: Water first, then shelter, then food
choices.description: Survival rule of three: 3 days without water.

choices.title: Build a large signal fire first
choices.description: Rescue priority, but neglect immediate needs.

choices.title: Search the island for more resources
choices.description: Explore before settling, but risk energy loss.

nodes.text: Day 2: A team member is injured. Another is panicking. You can only address one well.

choices.title: Tend the injury first, calm the panicker with tasks
choices.description: Medical priority, give the anxious person a job.

choices.title: Calm the panicker first, bandage the injury quickly
choices.description: Mental health priority, but wound may worsen.

choices.title: Tell them both to handle it themselves
choices.description: Save your energy for the group.

nodes.text: Day 5: Food is running low. You see fish, unknown berries, and birds. What do you eat?

choices.title: Fish only, berries tested in small amounts
Eat the berries, they look familiar
choices.description: Risk unknown food to save energy.

choices.title: Eat everything available

nodes.text: Day 7: A storm destroys your shelter and half your food supply.
After the storm: Low on food, no shelter, team morale is breaking. What now?

choices.title: Rebuild shelter together, ration food strictly, maintain hope
Send the strongest to search for rescue while others rest
Give up and wait for rescue

nodes.text: Day 10: A ship is visible on the horizon. You have one signal. What do you use?

choices.title: Large smoke signal during the day
Fire signal at night
choices.description: Visible at night, but wait is risky.

choices.title: Send the fastest swimmer

nodes.text: Rescue comes, or survival continues. Either way, you understand what keeps people alive.
reflection: What was more important than any single resource?
How did team trust affect survival?

intro: You have a small startup idea and a limited investment. Market, competition, and cash flow are real.
objective: Build a sustainable small business without compromising values.

nodes.text: Phase 1: You have 10,000. What is your first investment?

choices.title: Product quality and small test market
Big marketing campaign first
choices.description: Create buzz before product is perfect.

choices.title: Hire employees immediately
choices.description: Team first, but burn cash fast.

nodes.text: Phase 2: A competitor copies your idea and prices lower. What do you do?

choices.title: Improve quality and customer service
choices.description: Differentiate, do not compete on price.

choices.title: Lower your price to match
Copy their features and add more

nodes.text: Phase 3: A supplier offers cheaper materials if you hide quality issues from customers.

choices.title: Reject the offer, find ethical suppliers
Accept but improve quality later
Accept and keep silent

nodes.text: Phase 4: A major customer delays payment. Cash flow is critical.
Cash flow crisis: You cannot pay all bills. What do you prioritize?

choices.title: Pay employees first, negotiate with suppliers
Pay suppliers first, delay employee salaries
choices.description: Keep supply chain, risk team morale.

choices.title: Borrow from a high-interest lender

nodes.text: Phase 5: The business is stable. Where do you invest profits?

choices.title: Research and development for better product
Expand to new markets
Take profits and keep the business small

nodes.text: The business lives. The choices made were not just about money. They were about what kind of business you built.
reflection: What was more valuable than profit?
How did early ethics decisions shape the ending?

intro: You are managing a small rural clinic for a day. Patients, supplies, staff, and emergencies all arrive.
objective: Save lives, maintain staff health, and manage resources ethically.

nodes.text: Morning: Three patients arrive. One critical, two stable. You have one nurse. What do you do?

choices.title: Treat critical first, assign nurse to stable patients for monitoring
Treat critical first, make stable patients wait
choices.description: Correct triage, but wait may worsen.

choices.title: Treat stable patients first because they are easier

nodes.text: Midday: Medicine supply is low. You can order now (expensive) or wait for government supply (delayed).

choices.title: Order emergency stock, absorb cost
Wait for government supply, ration current stock
Buy cheap unverified medicine from local seller

nodes.text: Afternoon: A wealthy patient offers a large donation if treated first tomorrow. Others are waiting.

choices.title: Decline and maintain first-come-first-served
Accept but treat them within normal queue
Accept and give them priority

nodes.text: Evening: A car accident brings three injured people. Staff is exhausted.
Emergency: Staff is exhausted, but three lives are at risk. What do you do?

choices.title: Call all staff in, including off-duty, share the burden
Transfer stable cases to nearest hospital, focus on critical
Ask families to help care for their own

nodes.text: Night: The day is over. One final resource remains. What do you improve?

choices.title: Staff rest area and training
choices.description: Invest in people who save lives.

choices.title: Better equipment for faster diagnosis
Save the money for future emergencies

nodes.text: The clinic sleeps. Some patients recover. Some scars remain. You managed a system where every choice is a life.
reflection: How does resource scarcity test ethics?

intro: You are helping manage your family finances for a year. Income is limited. Needs are many.
objective: Balance savings, education, health, and emergencies over 12 months.

nodes.text: Month 1: Annual budget. What gets the largest share?

choices.title: Education and health first, then savings, then wants
Equal share for everything
Spend on immediate comfort and hope for more income

nodes.text: Month 3: A sibling needs a course for career growth. It costs 15,000. Budget is tight.

choices.title: Support the course, reduce discretionary spending
Suggest a loan they repay later
Say no, family needs come first

nodes.text: Month 6: A health emergency needs 20,000. You have savings, but it empties the buffer.

choices.title: Use savings, then rebuild buffer with small monthly cuts
Borrow from relatives
Delay treatment and hope it improves

nodes.text: Month 9: Income drops by 30% due to unexpected job loss.
Income is lower. How do you adapt the budget?

choices.title: Cut wants completely, protect education and health
Everyone reduces equally including education
Use credit cards and worry later

nodes.text: Month 12: Year end. You have a small surplus. What do you do?

choices.title: Start an emergency fund for the family
Celebrate together and share joy
Invest in a small side income source

nodes.text: The year ends. The family is together. Money was managed, but love was the real budget.
reflection: What was the most important financial decision?
How do family tradeoffs differ from individual ones?

intro: You are leading a local environmental initiative. Pollution, waste, and community habits are the enemies.
objective: Create sustainable change without alienating the community.

nodes.text: Phase 1: The river is polluted. Where do you start?

choices.title: Community cleanup drives and awareness
Report to government and wait for action
Install filters on your own

nodes.text: Phase 2: A factory is the main polluter. They offer sponsorship for your project if you stay silent.

choices.title: Reject and publicly advocate for cleaner production
Accept but use the money for green projects
Accept and stay silent

nodes.text: Phase 3: Waste segregation is failing. People mix everything. What do you change?

choices.title: Design simple color-coded bins and educate children
Fine households who do not segregate
Do nothing, habits change slowly

nodes.text: Phase 4: A flood damages your newly planted green belt.
Flood damage: The green belt is destroyed. What is your response?

choices.title: Replant with flood-resistant species, involve community
Rebuild exactly as before
Abandon the green belt project

nodes.text: Phase 5: One year later. What is your legacy project?

choices.title: Community-run compost and garden system
Solar energy for the community center
Annual awareness festival

nodes.text: The river is cleaner. The children know. The community remembers. Systems change slowly, but they change.
reflection: What was the hardest habit to change?
How does one person influence a system?

intro: A cyclone warning is issued. You have 24 hours to prepare your neighborhood.
objective: Save lives, protect property, and maintain order before and after the storm.

nodes.text: Hour 1: Warning received. What is your first action?

choices.title: Map vulnerable houses and assign volunteers
Warn everyone by loudspeaker and social media
Pack your own house first

nodes.text: Hour 6: Some families refuse to evacuate. Elders are stubborn. Children are scared.

choices.title: Send respected elders to convince them, offer support
Force evacuation with community volunteers
Leave them and focus on willing families

nodes.text: Hour 12: Supplies are short. You have food, water, medicine, but not enough for everyone.

choices.title: Prioritize children, elderly, and pregnant women
Distribute equally to all households
choices.description: Fair share, but vulnerable get less.

choices.title: Give more to families who helped in preparation

nodes.text: Hour 18: The storm hits harder than predicted. A roof collapses.
Crisis: Roof collapse, people injured, storm ongoing. What do you do?

choices.title: Organize rescue team, move injured to safe shelter, continue monitoring
Focus only on rescue, ignore storm monitoring
Wait for official rescue teams

nodes.text: Aftermath: Rebuilding begins. What is your first focus?

choices.title: Build a community emergency fund and stronger shelter
Rebuild exactly as before
Leave the neighborhood and move elsewhere

nodes.text: The storm passes. The community is scarred but standing. Some leaders are made in crisis.
reflection: What was the most urgent versus the most important decision?
